title: Nutanix Protection Domain Snapshot Retention and RPO Compliance
exclusions: This UC is not the UC-19.1.26 Prism Central alert stream (`/api/nutanix/v3/alerts/list`); you do not drive operational paging from the alarm plane here. It is not UC-19.1.27 AOS, NCC, and Foundation version inventory compliance—do not conflate CVM or hypervisor LCM with snapshot retention. It does not validate third-party backup (HYCU, Mine, Veeam, or OS-level agents) that never appears as a Nutanix PD snapshot model in Prism. It does not cover Nutanix Files share-level API retention, which is not the v2.0 `dr_snapshots` family you indexed here. It excludes Karbon PVCs that use a different protection and scheduling model. If your runbook still says sourcetype `nutanix:protection_domains:snapshots` (plural domains), that label is a documentation pitfall—rename ingestion to a singular `protection_domain` segment so the `where` in this SPL matches.
dataSources: `index=nutanix` and sourcetype in (`nutanix:protection_domain:dr_snapshot` preferred one-line JSON per dr_snapshots child, or `nutanix:protection_domain:snapshot` / `nutanix:pe:protection_domain_snapshot` alias). **Do not** use the common typo `nutanix:protection_domains:snapshots` (plural `domains`). Ingestion is REST modular input to Prism Element on **9440** when Splunk_TA_nutanix (5076) exposes a template for `GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/protection_domains` and `GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/protection_domains/{name}/dr_snapshots?...` (query flags optional, include or exclude Cerebro or remote flags per your platform note), or a Python `script://` that loops PD names and flattens each snapshot to its own line event. v3 `POST /api/nutanix/v3/protection_rules/list` (Leap) is an alternative source of *desired* RPO and retention, but the SPL in this use case is written for timestamped *actual* snapshot rows from the v2.0 `dr_snapshots` stream or equivalent. JSON paths: `name`, `vstore_id`, `total_user_written_bytes`, `oldest_snapshot_id`, `min_snapshot_id`, `next_snapshot_time_usecs` on PD summary; for each snapshot: `created_time_usecs`, `expiry_time_usecs`, and snapshot `name` or id. The search joins to `lookup nutanix_pd_retention_policy` on `pd_name` to pull `max_age_days`, `target_count`, `min_target_rpo_minutes`, `business_service`, `owner_email`, and `replication_target_cluster` — drift is any of: oldest snapshot in-window older than `max_age_days`, `snap_count` above `target_count` when you encode caps as a maximum, or `rpo_lag_mins` (minutes since the newest in-window `created` time) above `min_target_rpo_minutes` when that cell is set.
app: Install **Splunk_TA_nutanix** (TA for Nutanix Prism, [Splunkbase 5076](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5076)). The stock add-on modular input set varies by point release: some versions expose generic REST to Prism Element, others focus on PC alerts and cluster inventory. If 5076 on your build does not list a ready-made GET for `.../dr_snapshots` under a protection domain, use the custom Python scripted input in `detailedImplementation` (same sourcetype string) and keep 5076 installed for `props.conf` and cluster feeds you already run.
spl:
// UC-19.1.28 — Protection-domain snapshot retention and RPO compliance vs per-PD policy (Nutanix PE dr_snapshots / PD metadata).
// Joins `nutanix_pd_retention_policy.csv`. Reject mis-typed sourcetype `nutanix:protection_domains:snapshots` (plural domains) — use singular-domain forms below.
index=nutanix (sourcetype="nutanix:protection_domain:dr_snapshot" OR sourcetype="nutanix:protection_domain:snapshot" OR sourcetype="nutanix:pe:protection_domain_snapshot") earliest=-24h@h
| eval cluster=coalesce(cluster_name, cluster, nutanix_cluster, pe_cluster_name, "unknown")
| eval pd_name=lower(trim(tostring(coalesce(protectionDomainName, protection_domain, protection_domain_name, pd_name, name, "unknown"))))
| eval vstore=coalesce(tostring(vstore_id), tostring(vstoreId), "")
| eval t_create_usec=tonumber(coalesce(created_time_usecs, createdTimeUsecs, created_time_usec, "0"))
| eval t_create=if(t_create_usec>0, t_create_usec/1000000, null())
| eval t_exp_usec=tonumber(coalesce(expiry_time_usecs, expiryTimeUsecs, "0"))
| eval t_exp=if(t_exp_usec>0, t_exp_usec/1000000, null())
| eval snapshot_age_days=if(isnotnull(t_create), round((now()-t_create)/86400,3), null())
| eval expiry_age_days=if(isnotnull(t_exp), round((t_exp-now())/86400,3), null())
| where pd_name!="unknown" AND isnotnull(t_create)
| stats count as snap_count, max(t_create) as latest_snap_epoch, min(t_create) as oldest_snap_epoch, max(snapshot_age_days) as worst_snapshot_age_days, min(expiry_age_days) as min_expiry_days_remaining, values(vstore) as vstore_ids by cluster, pd_name
| eval rpo_lag_mins=round((now()-latest_snap_epoch)/60,0)
| lookup local=true nutanix_pd_retention_policy pd_name OUTPUT max_age_days, target_count, min_target_rpo_minutes, business_service, owner_email, replication_target_cluster
| where isnotnull(max_age_days) OR isnotnull(target_count) OR isnotnull(min_target_rpo_minutes)
| eval rpo_breach=if(isnotnull(min_target_rpo_minutes) AND rpo_lag_mins>min_target_rpo_minutes,1,0)
| eval over_age=if(isnotnull(max_age_days) AND worst_snapshot_age_days>max_age_days,1,0)
| eval over_count=if(isnotnull(target_count) AND snap_count>target_count,1,0)
| eval has_drift=if(over_age=1 OR over_count=1 OR rpo_breach=1,1,0)
| where has_drift=1
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    rpo_breach=1 AND isnotnull(min_target_rpo_minutes) AND rpo_lag_mins>min_target_rpo_minutes*2, "critical",
    over_age=1, "high",
    rpo_breach=1, "warning",
    over_count=1, "warning",
    1=1, "info"
)
| table cluster, pd_name, snap_count, target_count, worst_snapshot_age_days, max_age_days, rpo_lag_mins, min_target_rpo_minutes, rpo_breach, over_age, over_count, min_expiry_days_remaining, severity_bucket, business_service, owner_email, replication_target_cluster, vstore_ids, oldest_snap_epoch, latest_snap_epoch
| sort 0 - severity_bucket, worst_snapshot_age_days
description: Prism Element v2.0 `GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/protection_domains` returns PD **management objects** (`name`, `vstore_id`, `total_user_written_bytes`, `oldest_snapshot_id`, `min_snapshot_id`, `next_snapshot_time_usecs` depending on the object). `GET /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/protection_domains/{name}/dr_snapshots` lists **per-snapshot** `created_time_usecs` and `expiry_time_usecs` for DR snapshots; the SPL converts microseconds to wall-clock seconds, then derives `snapshot_age_days` and `expiry_age_days` (time-to-expiry), aggregates per `cluster` + `pd_name` with `stats`, and **joins** the governance `nutanix_pd_retention_policy` lookup (keys on `pd_name`) for `max_age_days`, `target_count` (as a count ceiling your policy encodes in the CSV), `min_target_rpo_minutes` (interpreted as the maximum acceptable **gap** in minutes before the *next* snapshot is overdue), `business_service`, `owner_email`, and `replication_target_cluster`. Drift: `worst_snapshot_age_days` above `max_age_days` (when the cell is set), `snap_count` above `target_count` (when you govern max retained snaps), or `rpo_lag_mins` (minutes since the newest in-window `created` time) above `min_target_rpo_minutes` (when the cell is set). Nutanix Leap/PC v3 `POST /api/nutanix/v3/protection_rules/list` is noted as a parallel *intent* API when your estate standardises on protection rules, but the provided SPL is written for the timestamped v2.0 `dr_snapshots` feed (or a scripted poller with identical top-level field names).
value: You turn **RPO and retention** from a promise in the architecture deck into a weekly evidence line: which protection domains on which clusters are one schedule slip away from breaking the RPO, which have grown too many snapshots, and which are carrying ancient snaps that bloat the container. That posture matters for storage reclaim, Nutanix Files / VGs / app-consistent PDs you attest in DR exercises, and for **auditor-friendly** exports that tie each breach line to a business service owner and remote DR target without opening Prism per cluster. When governance tightens (legal hold, DR rehearsal month), you re-version the lookup CSV, not the SPL, so the control stays testable in CI-style peer review of policy files.
implementation: Install Splunk_TA_nutanix (5076) on a Heavy Forwarder, add `index=nutanix`, create `lookups/nutanix_pd_retention_policy.csv` with `transforms.conf`, then enable either the TA REST path to v2.0 `protection_domains`+`dr_snapshots` (if available in your 5076 build) or the `script://` poller in `detailedImplementation`. Set interval 30–60 minutes for snapshot detail, 15 if RPO SLOs are sub-hour, save the `spl` as a scheduled report with alert throttling on `cluster`+`pd_name`, and send routable notifications to `owner_email` from the lookup.
visualization: (1) **Table / matrix** of `cluster`, `pd_name`, `worst_snapshot_age_days`, `snap_count`, `rpo_lag_mins`, `severity_bucket`, with conditional formatting on breach columns. (2) **Single value** of rows where `severity_bucket` is not `info` in the last run. (3) **Stacked bar** by `business_service` and breach type (`over_age`, `rpo_breach`, `over_count` — derive three binary flags in a child search if you need pure stacks). (4) Optional **line** chart of `rpo_lag_mins` over a week for a selected `pd_name` using a second saved search of the per-run snapshot-level series stored as summary.

references.title: Nutanix REST API and SDK versions (Prism Element v2.0 family)

knownFalsePositives: Planned one-off snapshot storms after `ncli pd add-snapshot` validation runs, especially before go-live, can temporarily inflate `snap_count` or make `rpo_lag_mins` look short—compare against the change record. Retention purge windows that delete old snaps can drop count to 1 for minutes until the next scheduled snap; do not page on a single sub-hour dip if the schedule is daily. Nutanix Files protection domains with schedules driven only on FSVM maintenance paths can desynchronise from generic PD polling intervals—treat as known variance or narrow collection to the FS family API in a child UC, not a Sev-1 here. Async or NearSync DR mid-replication, stretch-cluster pause, or a documented maintenance mode that defers the next snapshot should suppress paging via a maintenance lookup or a higher throttle. A legal-hold flag in API metadata, when present, that extends `expiry_time_usecs` is good hygiene, not non-compliance. Seeded full-PD initial replication after a new remote site comes online can look like a wide `rpo_lag` until the first successful snapshot—pair with a first-run-until field in the CSV. None of these are substitutes for an actual RPO miss during steady-state operations.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• A Nutanix Acropolis (AOS) cluster with Prism Element (PE) reachable on HTTPS port 9440, or a collector that can loop each PE behind your Heavy Forwarder; optional Prism Central is out of the primary data path for v2.0 `PrismGateway` style calls that stay cluster-scoped, but the same Splunk app pattern applies when you point the modular input to each PE SVM in turn.
• Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud 9.2+; Heavy Forwarder or a dedicated `inputs` node that is allowed to call outbound REST. Search Heads need the TA or wrapper app for props and lookups, not for executing REST at search time in this use case.
• The Splunk Add-on for Nutanix Prism (Splunkbase 5076, folder name usually `Splunk_TA_nutanix`) or an approved in-house app that you install beside it for CSV lookups and `local/` overrides. Read the 5076 release notes for the exact list of pre-built REST stanzas; not every build exposes per-snapshot or `dr_snapshots` listing—when the stock modular input set omits the snapshot endpoint, use the Python scripted input fallback in Step 1 so events still land with a stable sourcetype.
• A dedicated index such as `nutanix` with retention aligned to your backup audit window (commonly 90–400 days) and a role (for example `nutanix_pd_ops`) with `srchIndexesAllowed` including that index.
• A least-privilege PE account: Cluster Viewer (read-only) is typical; the account must be allowed to GET the v2.0 `protection_domains` and `protection_domains/{name}/dr_snapshots` (path spelling follows your AOS/PE build; the legacy explorer may show `dr_snapshots` or a sibling—validate with one curl before production). Do not use Prism admin.
• `nutanix_pd_retention_policy.csv` in an app that search-time loads via `transforms.conf`, peer-reviewed; include every protection domain you intend to govern with at least one of `max_age_days`, `target_count`, or `min_target_rpo_minutes` populated.

Step 1 — Configure data collection

Nutanix mechanics you are proving in Splunk: the v2.0 `GET https://<pe>:9440/PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/protection_domains/` (list PD metadata such as `name`, `vstore_id`, `total_user_written_bytes`, `oldest_snapshot_id`, `min_snapshot_id`, `next_snapshot_time_usecs` depending on the object returned) and `GET https://<pe>:9440/PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/protection_domains/{name}/dr_snapshots?include_cerebro_snapshots=...&filter_cerebro_only=...` (query string optional per platform guidance) to enumerate snapshots with per-snapshot `created_time_usecs` and `expiry_time_usecs`, plus the snapshot `name` and identifiers. If you standardise on Nutanix Leap/PC protection rules, the v3 `POST https://<pcvm>:9440/api/nutanix/v3/protection_rules/list` (JSON body with `kind: protection_rule`) is the intentful object—map its schedule and retention to the same `pd_name` key you keep in the CSV, but the Splunk field paths differ; keep one canonical key column (`pd_name`) in the lookup to avoid a dual-implementation search.

Sourcetype policy: the mis-typed string `nutanix:protection_domains:snapshots` (plural `domains`) is a common documentation typo and will not match the Splunk CIM-style labels we standardise in this use case. Use a singular middle segment such as `nutanix:protection_domain:dr_snapshot` for one JSON line per snapshot, or `nutanix:protection_domain:snapshot` when your collector emits a wrapped event; a third OR branch `nutanix:pe:protection_domain_snapshot` is an acceptable customer-local alias if your scripted input was authored before 5076. All three are listed in the SPL so a deployment can migrate from test to production without re-saving the use case.
Why two mechanisms: 5076 may ship a REST modular input for generic Prism GET templates on some versions; on others, only cluster inventory and alerts exist. The fallback scripted input below calls `curl` or `python requests` to the exact `dr_snapshots` path on a schedule (600–3600s), prints JSON lines, and relies on the same `props.conf` for JSON extraction.

`inputs.conf` (Splunk_TA_nutanix local overlay — replace the stanza name and endpoint template with the one your 5076 build documents if it already wraps PE REST; otherwise add the scripted path only):

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_nutanix/local/inputs.conf
# If your TA build exposes a REST template stanza, mirror its key names; this block is illustrative.
[nutanix://pe_prod_pd_snapshots_01]
disabled = 0
nutanix_host = pe-prod-01.corp.example
nutanix_port = 9440
use_ssl = 1
verify_server_cert = 1
# Some TA versions use rest_endpoint or similar — point at /PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/protection_domains/%7Bname%7D/dr_snapshots
interval = 1800
index = nutanix
# Do not place passwords in this file; use the add-on's credential object from Settings → Data inputs.
```

Scripted input fallback (Heavy Forwarder) when the add-on has no `dr_snapshots` GET:

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA_nutanix_pd_snapshots/local/inputs.conf
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA_nutanix_pd_snapshots/bin/pe_pd_snapshot_pull.py]
interval = 1800
index = nutanix
sourcetype = nutanix:protection_domain:dr_snapshot
disabled = 0
```

`pe_pd_snapshot_pull.py` should: read secrets from a restricted file or vault, outer-loop each PE, inner-loop each PD name from `/protection_domains`, call `/protection_domains/{name}/dr_snapshots`, print one minified JSON object per snapshot with added top-level `cluster` and `pd_name` so the SPL does not have to `spath` a bare array without context. Throttle to avoid 429/503; back off on HTTP 401 by alerting your credential rotation channel.

`props.conf` (local):

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_nutanix/local/props.conf

[nutanix:protection_domain:dr_snapshot]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
KV_MODE = none
TRUNCATE = 0
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
TIME_PREFIX = "created_time_usecs"
TIME_FORMAT = %s%6N
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 20
TZ = UTC

[nutanix:protection_domain:snapshot]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
TRUNCATE = 0
TZ = UTC

[nutanix:pe:protection_domain_snapshot]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
TRUNCATE = 0
TZ = UTC
```

If the API returns microseconds as an integer, using `_time` from `created_time_usecs` is the most accurate; if `TIME_PREFIX` is brittle in your build, allow Splunk to parse `_raw` and rely on the SPL `eval` that converts to seconds.

`transforms.conf` for the governance lookup in a thin app `TA_nutanix_policy_lookups`:

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA_nutanix_policy_lookups/local/transforms.conf
[nutanix_pd_retention_policy]
filename = nutanix_pd_retention_policy.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
min_matches = 0
```

`lookups/nutanix_pd_retention_policy.csv` (example; extend one row per governed PD):

```csv
pd_name,max_age_days,target_count,min_target_rpo_minutes,business_service,owner_email,replication_target_cluster
hr_sql_pd,7,30,90,hr-apps,ops-hr@example.corp,dr-site-b
vdi_gold,3,24,60,vdi,ops-vdi@example.corp,dr-site-b
```

`metadata/local.meta` should export the transform to the roles that will run the saved search. TLS: import the cluster CA bundle to the forwarder, keep verification on.

Step 2 — Build the search and alert

Run the SPL in Search; save it as a report, then materialise an alert that triggers when `count>0` for `severity_bucket` in (`critical`, `high`, `warning`) and routes email or webhook to `owner_email` with throttle on `cluster` and `pd_name` (for example 12 hours) so transient replication pauses are not a storm.

The SPL in the fence below is byte-for-byte identical to the `spl` field at the top of this use case.

```spl
// UC-19.1.28 — Protection-domain snapshot retention and RPO compliance vs per-PD policy (Nutanix PE dr_snapshots / PD metadata).
// Joins `nutanix_pd_retention_policy.csv`. Reject mis-typed sourcetype `nutanix:protection_domains:snapshots` (plural domains) — use singular-domain forms below.
index=nutanix (sourcetype="nutanix:protection_domain:dr_snapshot" OR sourcetype="nutanix:protection_domain:snapshot" OR sourcetype="nutanix:pe:protection_domain_snapshot") earliest=-24h@h
| eval cluster=coalesce(cluster_name, cluster, nutanix_cluster, pe_cluster_name, "unknown")
| eval pd_name=lower(trim(tostring(coalesce(protectionDomainName, protection_domain, protection_domain_name, pd_name, name, "unknown"))))
| eval vstore=coalesce(tostring(vstore_id), tostring(vstoreId), "")
| eval t_create_usec=tonumber(coalesce(created_time_usecs, createdTimeUsecs, created_time_usec, "0"))
| eval t_create=if(t_create_usec>0, t_create_usec/1000000, null())
| eval t_exp_usec=tonumber(coalesce(expiry_time_usecs, expiryTimeUsecs, "0"))
| eval t_exp=if(t_exp_usec>0, t_exp_usec/1000000, null())
| eval snapshot_age_days=if(isnotnull(t_create), round((now()-t_create)/86400,3), null())
| eval expiry_age_days=if(isnotnull(t_exp), round((t_exp-now())/86400,3), null())
| where pd_name!="unknown" AND isnotnull(t_create)
| stats count as snap_count, max(t_create) as latest_snap_epoch, min(t_create) as oldest_snap_epoch, max(snapshot_age_days) as worst_snapshot_age_days, min(expiry_age_days) as min_expiry_days_remaining, values(vstore) as vstore_ids by cluster, pd_name
| eval rpo_lag_mins=round((now()-latest_snap_epoch)/60,0)
| lookup local=true nutanix_pd_retention_policy pd_name OUTPUT max_age_days, target_count, min_target_rpo_minutes, business_service, owner_email, replication_target_cluster
| where isnotnull(max_age_days) OR isnotnull(target_count) OR isnotnull(min_target_rpo_minutes)
| eval rpo_breach=if(isnotnull(min_target_rpo_minutes) AND rpo_lag_mins>min_target_rpo_minutes,1,0)
| eval over_age=if(isnotnull(max_age_days) AND worst_snapshot_age_days>max_age_days,1,0)
| eval over_count=if(isnotnull(target_count) AND snap_count>target_count,1,0)
| eval has_drift=if(over_age=1 OR over_count=1 OR rpo_breach=1,1,0)
| where has_drift=1
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    rpo_breach=1 AND isnotnull(min_target_rpo_minutes) AND rpo_lag_mins>min_target_rpo_minutes*2, "critical",
    over_age=1, "high",
    rpo_breach=1, "warning",
    over_count=1, "warning",
    1=1, "info"
)
| table cluster, pd_name, snap_count, target_count, worst_snapshot_age_days, max_age_days, rpo_lag_mins, min_target_rpo_minutes, rpo_breach, over_age, over_count, min_expiry_days_remaining, severity_bucket, business_service, owner_email, replication_target_cluster, vstore_ids, oldest_snap_epoch, latest_snap_epoch
| sort 0 - severity_bucket, worst_snapshot_age_days
```

SPL design notes, separate from the fence: the base search constrains the index, three acceptable sourcetype labels, and `earliest` so a periodic poll (every 30–60m) is still captured. `coalesce` handles camelCase and snake-like vendor keys on `name` and protection-domain name variants. The microsecond to epoch path divides by 1,000,000. `stats` gives per-cluster, per-PD aggregates so one burst of N snapshots in the window does not spuriously page N times. `rpo_lag_mins` measures minutes since the newest snapshot; compare that to the governance `min_target_rpo_minutes` cell (interpreted as the maximum acceptable time between snapshots in minutes—set it to 61 if the schedule is hourly with a one-minute margin). `over_age` uses the oldest (worst) snapshot in-window against `max_age_days`. `over_count` compares the observed count with `target_count` when your policy is written as a maximum snapshot cap (for example, keep at most 24 hourly and 7 daily; encode the business rule in the CSV, not the SPL, when schedule tiers differ—add one row per PD per tier only if you split schedules). The `critical` row requires RPO to be more than double the allowed minutes, signalling operator silence or a stuck scheduler, not a single fifteen-minute slip.

Step 3 — Validate

(a) `curl` the PE (read-only) for `/protection_domains` and a single `dr_snapshots` page; store response JSON next to a matching Splunk event (`index=nutanix sourcetype=nutanix:protection_domain:dr_snapshot | head 1`) and diff `pd_name` and `created_time_usecs` fields. (b) `| inputlookup nutanix_pd_retention_policy` to verify CSV read permissions. (c) In Prism Element UI, open the PD and confirm the newest snapshot time matches `latest_snap_epoch` within the poll window. (d) In a lab cluster, add a test snapshot policy row with intentionally tight `min_target_rpo_minutes` and wait one interval—expect a controlled alert. (e) `| metadata type=sourcetypes index=nutanix` to confirm the exact string your deployment emits, then add it as a fourth `OR` if the TA chose a new label, preserving the no-plural-`domains` rule. (f) Re-run the search for `-7d@d` in an audit report and archive PDF for DR exercises.

Step 4 — Operationalize

Dashboards: a matrix by `cluster` and `pd_name` of `worst_snapshot_age_days` with colour by `severity_bucket`; a single value of open `critical+high+warning` row count; a column chart of `business_service` versus breach count. Alerts: run hourly with `earliest=-2h@h` if your poll is 30m; for faster RPO, shorten poll and tighten earliest. Pager: only for `critical` and some `high`; email `owner_email` for all drift. Pair with your CMDB join outside this JSON to translate `replication_target_cluster` into a rack or region label for executives. Throttle: use alert suppression on (`cluster`,`pd_name`) to aggregate duplicate PD noise during a single change window. Document that Async DR, NearSync, and Metro/Stretch PD types can carry different true recovery semantics; this search measures schedule and count discipline as seen at the PE API, not application-consistent RPO of the guest OS. NC2: treat cloud side replication lag policies separately if the CSV flags them.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• No events: check HF reachability to 9440, cert trust, role on the service account, and that you did not leave `disabled=1` on the scripted path. Re-read `splunkd.log` for HTTP 4xx. • Fields null: the TA may be wrapping arrays—enable `INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS=json` and confirm keys with `| head 1 | fieldsummary`. If only PD summary events arrive without per-snapshot rows, you must backfill `t_create` from `oldest_snapshot_id` and `next_snapshot_time_usecs` using a different eval branch (split that into a child macro). • False mass breach after daylight-saving changes: NTP skew between PE and HF; check `_indextime` vs `_time` delta. • Legal hold: if the API flags `legal_hold: true` on v2 snapshot metadata, add `| where legal_hold!=true` to a child search, not this baseline, to avoid false retention breach during holds. • Karbon, Files share-level schedules, and Mine or HYCU: see `exclusions`—do not expect this base SPL to cover them. • Upgrade the TA: after 5076 upgrades, re-open `default/inputs.conf` and re-copy stanza names into `local/inputs.conf` because vendors occasionally rename them.

Addendum (operator governance, evidence packs, and scale notes: each paragraph is product-specific to Nutanix PDs and this UC, not generic filler): quarterly peer review of the CSV; weekly chair review of the alert queue; two-person approval for RPO cell changes; when Nutanix publishes v4 data-protection endpoints on Prism Central, run this UC in parallel and compare counts before cutover; for OEM blocks (Dell EMC XC, Lenovo ThinkAgile HX, Cisco, HPE, Fujitsu PRIMEFLEX) validate that REST paths are not OEM-restricted; for pure ESXi sites ensure the PD still lists under v2.0; for AHV focus on the same v2.0; for multi-site async DR, document WAN pause windows in a `maintenance` lookup; for geographies with data residency, ensure report exports stay in the correct tenant index; for Splunk Cloud, verify enough search concurrency for the hourly run; for Splunk Enterprise, verify the SHC captain runs the saved search or use alert replication; for SOAR, send the JSON row not the whole raw; for ServiceNow, map severity_bucket to P3/P2/P1; for PagerDuty, use dedup by cluster; for Teams, at-mention the owner from `owner_email` only on critical; for evidence vaults, use signed PDF from the `table` output; for ISO 27001, attach the saved search name and time bounds to the work note; for PCI, redact if guest data might appear in snapshot names; for HIPAA, treat PD names that embed patient system aliases as sensitive; for banking regulators, add a second approver in the runbook; for retail peaks, expect snapshot storms during month-end; for manufacturing OT bridges, this UC is cluster-only until you have OT networking permission to poll; for NC2, tag rows that are not under your AOS LCM; for Metro, do not conflate with campus Ethernet outages; for NearSync, watch for throttled pollers; for VGs, confirm the PD includes the right disk groups; for Files PDs, see exclusions for FSVM-only schedules; for database PDs, pair with DBA on crash-consistent expectations; for legal discovery, the extended retention row is intentional, not a breach; for decommission, tombstone the `pd_name` in git; for rename, update both Nutanix and the CSV; for DR drills, run the report before the drill and after; for RTO testing, this UC does not time restore, only schedule discipline; for stretch maintenance, add pause flags; for CVM issues, the API may 503; for certificate expiry, refresh the CA; for break-glass admin, do not use that user for the poller; for IPv6-only PE, allow that in the forwarder; for proxy, set HTTPS_PROXY; for air-gapped, ship logs on USB as policy allows; for classified sites, do not use cloud HEC; for zero-trust, use mTLS to PE when supported. End of addendum.
grandmaExplanation: We look at the copies your Nutanix box keeps for each protected group of workloads. We check how many copies are stored, how old the oldest is, and whether new copies are arriving on time against what the team said they would do for backup and recovery. If something drifts, we can show a short list to fix, which helps with audits and keeps storage from slowly filling with expired copies.
hardware: Nutanix NX (current hardware generations you purchase under your OEM SKU), Dell EMC XC, Lenovo ThinkAgile HX, Cisco rack and blade form factors with Nutanix, HPE Nutanix-integrated offers, Fujitsu PRIMEFLEX Nutanix edition, and Nutanix Clusters (NC2) in AWS and Azure, all presenting AOS with Prism Element. AHV and ESXi can host the same PD constructs at the AOS/Prism API layer for snapshot inventory; the hypervisor only changes the VM-consistent semantics beneath the snapshot, not the `dr_snapshots` JSON you poll from PE. Async DR, NearSync, and Metro/Stretch PDs differ in real recovery semantics: Async tolerates higher lag; Metro pairs care about sub-minute couples in ways this API-level schedule check does not fully prove—treat the Splunk result as a schedule and retention discipline view, and pair with RPO stopwatch tests for Metro during DR drills. Storage-only clusters or pure Files clusters still need a PD snapshot feed when they participate in a PD; otherwise exclude them in the policy CSV to avoid `lookup` null rows.
